2. **Landable narrower lever = exact-integer fast path.** For an integral value with |x| < 2^64 (very common: counts, sizes, whole-dollar %.2f), the decimal expansion is exact — format `x as u64` digits + '.' + N zeros, no flt2dec. Byte-identical (integral ⇒ fractional part exactly 0); helps %f and %g-fixed. Reproducer `intf_iso` committed (integer-format vs Rust `{:.N}`; std flt2dec ~90ns vs trivial u64 digits ⇒ expected large win for integral inputs — a same-worker number could not be captured this turn due to an rch stdout-flush issue on the bench, re-run in the dedicated turn). RISK to weigh next turn: bench-overfit (only helps integral doubles; printf_float_differential_fuzz uses random non-integral doubles so add targeted integer conformance cases) + edge care (-0.0, precision 0, %g strip, alt_form, x>=2^64 must fall through). Consistent with the existing bare-%f / literal printf fast-path precedent.
Reproducers: `e_iso` (Rust {:.6} vs {:.6e}), `intf_iso` (integer fast path A/B).

## 2026-07-04 (BlackThrush) — WIN (SHIPPED): printf %f exact-integer fast path, 4.80x for integral values, byte-identical
Landed the "exact-integer fast path" scoped in the entry above. A finite integral `abs` < 2^64 has an EXACT fixed-point form (integer digits + `precision` zeros), so `%f` can skip Rust std's flt2dec entirely. Hooked both deployed %f render sites in printf.rs: the bare-%f fast path (precision>=1, width==0 — the hot common case) and `format_f` (width-padded / general). Guard: `abs.fract()==0.0 && abs < 2^64` — `fract()==0.0` excludes inf/nan (already returned earlier anyway) and proves integral; `< 2^64` makes `as u64` exact. Emits `u64` digits + '.' + zeros. Byte-identical to `{:.prec$}` for integral values (the fractional part is exactly 0); non-integral / >=2^64 fall through unchanged. **Measured 4.80x (`intf_iso` in-process A/B: Rust `{:.N}`=114.2ns vs u64-digits+pad=23.8ns, new/old=0.208), byte-identical over 12 integral cases incl -0.0/prec0/1e15/negatives.** CONFORMANCE GREEN vs host glibc: `printf_float_differential_fuzz` (its value pool includes integer values crossed with random flags/width/precision/alt-form) PASS + `conformance_diff_printf_fastpaths` (3) + `conformance_diff_printf_hexfloat` (1). This is a REAL deployed win for a common real-world pattern (counts/sizes/whole-dollar `%.2f`), not bench-overfit-only: integral %f is genuine hot traffic and glibc runs full gdtoa for it. The general dtoa gap (non-integral, entry above) still needs the multi-turn Schubfach port. Reproducer: `intf_iso`.

## 2026-07-04 (BlackThrush) — WIN (SHIPPED): render_pct_e (%e / strfromd) exact-integer fast path, 3.40x, byte-identical
Extended the exact-integer float vein (shipped %f last commit) to `%e` by hooking `render_pct_e` (ecvt.rs) — the shared %e core used by printf `%e`/`%E` (non-alt) AND `strfromd` %e (the worst float gap, 3.65x vs glibc). A finite integral |value| < 2^64 whose integer has at most `ndigit+1` significant digits (`digit_count - 1 <= ndigit`, i.e. NO rounding) formats directly to glibc's e-form: first digit, '.', remaining integer digits, zero-pad to ndigit, `e±dd` (exponent = digit_count-1). Stack-buffer itoa, one output alloc, skips Rust std `{:.Ne}` flt2dec + the reshape. Guard `fract()==0.0` (excludes inf/nan/non-integral → std path unchanged) `&& abs < 2^64` (exact `as u64`) `&& digit_count-1 <= ndigit` (no rounding ⇒ byte-exact). **Measured 3.40x (`inte_iso` in-process A/B: Rust `{:.Ne}`+reshape=144.8ns vs int-build=42.5ns, new/old=0.294), byte-identical over 14 cases incl 0/-0.0/prec0/1e15/trailing-zero(1200)/negatives.** CONFORMANCE GREEN vs host glibc: `printf_float_differential_fuzz` (integer values × %e/%E specs) PASS + `strfromd_differential_fuzz` + `strfromf_differential_fuzz` (exit 0). Common in scientific/engineering %e output; helps the worst-gap strfromd %e for integral inputs. Non-integral / rounding-needed / >=2^64 fall through to std (still 2-3.7x until the Schubfach port). Reproducer: `inte_iso`.

## 2026-07-04 (BlackThrush) — WIN (SHIPPED): render_gcvt (%g) sci-render exact-integer fast path, 3.95x, byte-identical
Completed the exact-integer float vein for `%g` (after %f + %e). `render_gcvt` already fast-paths fixed-style small integers via `try_gcvt_exact_small_fixed`; the remaining gap is the `{value:.frac$e}` probe render (flt2dec) that %g does to pick fixed-vs-scientific and to build scientific-style / >=2^53 integer output. Added `try_build_integer_sci`: for a finite integral |value| < 2^64 with at most `frac+1` significant digits (guard `digit_count-1 <= frac`, no rounding), it writes Rust's `{value:.frac$e}` form BYTE-FOR-BYTE (integer digits, exp = digit_count-1, `frac` zero-padded fractional slots, bare Rust exponent) via a stack itoa — so ALL the tested %g downstream logic (exponent extract, fixed-vs-scientific choice, trailing-zero strip) runs unchanged on identical bytes. Non-integral / rounding-needed fall back to flt2dec. **Measured 3.95x (`gsci_iso` in-process A/B: Rust `{:.Fe}`=130.3ns vs int-build=33.0ns, new/old=0.253), byte-identical to `{:.frac$e}` over 15 cases (fixed+scientific ranges, 0/-0/1e6/1.2e6/1e15/1e18/negatives).** CONFORMANCE GREEN vs host glibc: `printf_float_differential_fuzz` (integer values × %g specs) + `strfromd_differential_fuzz` + `conformance_gcvt_byte_stable`. Helps printf `%g` (default float format) + strfromd %g (2.65x gap) + `gcvt()` for scientific/large integral inputs. **Float-integer vein now complete across %f/%e/%g.** General non-integral %e/%f/%g still needs the Schubfach port. Reproducer: `gsci_iso`.

## 2026-07-04 (BlackThrush) — WIN (SHIPPED): memcmp AVX2 head-peel to 32-align s1, both-unaligned 1.5x -> ~parity
Landed the alignment-peel scoped 3 entries above (the memcmp 1.5x in-L1 gap = cache-line-split penalty from loading BOTH operands unaligned; glibc peels one pointer). The 128B all-equal asm loop loaded `s1` via `vmovdqu` and folded `s2` into `vpcmpeqb ymm,ymm,[s2+i]` — both unaligned, so every 32B load can straddle a 64B line. Fix: when `n >= 128`, compare the first 32B unaligned (covers the sub-32 head), then start the loop at the next 32-aligned `s1` offset (`start = (32 - (s1 & 31)) & 31`) and use `vmovdqa` for the four `s1` loads (a 32-aligned 32B load never crosses a 64B line); `s2` stays the unaligned `vpcmpeqb` memory operand → only one operand can split. Byte-identical (`[0,32)` pre-compared, `start<=31<32` so the loop's first window re-covers `[start,32)` with no gap; page-safe because alignment rounds UP so all loads stay in `[0,n)`). **CORRECTNESS: `memcmp_ab` 4940 sign checks fl==glibc + `conformance_diff_cmp_family` 5/5.** **PERF (`memcmp_align`, fl-vs-glibc in-process ratio, worker-independent): `both+16` (both operands at offset 16, the worst split case) n=4096 1.5x->1.044x, n=16384 1.5x->1.010x = near-parity; `aligned32`/`skew` unchanged (~1.03-1.06x, no regression); small n<128 untouched (extern-call floor).** memcmp is a top-hot libc fn; this closes the split-penalty gap glibc's one-pointer-align avoids. Reproducers: `memcmp_align` (perf by alignment), `memcmp_ab` (verify+time).

## 2026-07-04 (BlackThrush) — WIN (SHIPPED): memcpy dest-align peel, unaligned-dest 1.32x LOSS -> WIN
Applied the memcmp-peel insight to memcpy (`raw_avx_copy`): its 128B loop used `vmovdqu` for BOTH loads and STORES, so an unaligned `dst` splits every 32B store across a 64B cache line. glibc aligns the destination. Measured gap (`memcpy_align`, new reproducer, fl-vs-glibc in-process): unaligned-dst LOSES (n=16384 dst+16 **1.322x**, both+16 1.206x) while aligned-dst is parity-to-win (src+16 0.973x) — dest alignment IS the gap. FIX (separate code path, ORIGINAL untouched ⇒ zero regression on aligned/small): when `dst` is unaligned and `head+128 <= n` (n large enough to keep the aligned loop's mandatory first 128B iter in bounds), copy the first 32B unaligned (covers the sub-32 head), then run an aligned-STORE loop (`vmovdqa [dst_aligned]`) from the next 32-aligned dst offset; `src` stays `vmovdqu` (load splits cheaper, only one operand needs aligning). Byte-identical (disjoint copy; `[head,32)` re-written with same data by the loop's first store; all offsets in `[0,n)`). **PERF (`memcpy_align`): n=16384 dst+16 1.322x->0.904x (LOSS->WIN!), both+16 1.206x->0.972x; n=4096 dst+16 0.919x->0.770x, both+16 0.911x->0.726x; aligned-dst (dstalign/src+16) UNCHANGED (peel not triggered).** CORRECTNESS: `memcpy_align` asserts OK + `conformance_diff_memcpy`/`_memmove`/`_mempcpy_rawmemchr` all green vs glibc. memcpy is the hottest libc fn; this closes the store-side split penalty for unaligned dst. Residual: aligned-dst n=16384 still ~1.11x (glibc's large-copy rep-movsb/NT-store advantage, separate). memmove's `raw_avx_copy_forward` has the same shape (follow-up). Reproducer: `memcpy_align`.

## 2026-07-04 (BlackThrush) — LOSS/DROP: memset dest-align peel is ~0-gain (fl already parity-to-win); memmove-overlap peel deferred (low-EV)
After the memcmp + memcpy alignment-peel wins, checked the two remaining AVX2 kernels with unaligned `vmovdqu` stores for the same cache-line-split lever. **memset (`raw_avx_memset`) — DROPPED, ~0-gain.** `memset_align` (new reproducer, fl-vs-glibc in-process): fl memset is ALREADY parity-to-win at EVERY alignment — n=16384 dstalign 1.037x / dst+16 **0.986x**; n=4096 dstalign 0.972x / dst+16 0.971x / dst+3 0.972x / dst+1 0.971x. Unlike memcpy (which LOST 1.32x on unaligned dst), memset has NO unaligned-dst penalty vs glibc: glibc doesn't align-peel memset either, and a broadcast-value store split is far cheaper than memcpy's load+store split (no load side, write-combining absorbs it). So a dest-align peel on memset would recover nothing. NOT implemented (measure-first avoided a useless change to the hottest libc fn). Residual: memset dstalign n=16384 1.037x is a mild large-fill gap (glibc NT-store / rep-stosb tuning), same class as the memcpy dstalign 1.11x residual — not a clean peel target. **memmove-overlap (`raw_avx_copy_forward`/`_backward`) — DEFERRED, low-EV.** Disjoint memmove already routes to the peeled `raw_avx_copy`; these two are reached ONLY for GENUINE overlap (a minority of memmove calls). The forward peel is also correctness-subtle: the 32B head store clobbers src bytes the main loop needs unless overlap distance `src-dst >= 32`, so it needs a distance guard. Narrow slice × correctness risk ⇒ not worth a focused turn. **Alignment-peel vein's HIGH-VALUE targets (memcmp, memcpy-disjoint) are DONE; memset is a confirmed non-target; memmove-overlap is the low-EV remainder.** Reproducer: `memset_align`.

## 2026-07-04 (BlackThrush) — WIN (SHIPPED): memmove forward-overlap dest-align peel, unaligned-dst 1.31x -> parity (corrects prior "low-EV" note)
CORRECTION to the memset/memmove entry above (which deferred memmove-overlap as "low-EV" ASSUMING overlap is negligible): MEASURED it (`memmove_fwd_align`, new reproducer, forward overlap dst<src shift=64) and it IS a real ~1.3x gap — unaligned-dst forward-overlap memmove loses n=4096 dst+16 **1.307x**, dst+8 1.293x vs aligned 1.024x (same store-split penalty as memcpy; glibc aligns the dest). Applied the dest-align peel to `raw_avx_copy_forward`, gated to preserve ascending overlap correctness: peel only when `dst+32 <= src` (overlap distance >= 32, so the 32B head store `[dst,dst+32)` never clobbers a source byte the main loop still needs) AND dst unaligned AND head+128<=n. Copy the head ascending, aligned-STORE loop (`vmovdqa`) from the next 32-aligned dst, same ascending remainder. Small-distance overlaps (src-dst < 32) keep the original unaligned path (a head store there would corrupt src). **PERF (`memmove_fwd_align`): n=4096 dst+16 1.307x->1.089x, dst+8 1.293x->1.172x — unaligned now ~parity with the aligned level (peel eliminated the split penalty).** CORRECTNESS: `memmove_fwd_align` byte-wise asserts OK for head=16 AND head=29 peel paths (od=16/3, shift=64, up to n=16384) + `conformance_diff_memmove` green (covers small-distance overlaps where the peel is correctly skipped). Disjoint memmove already used the peeled `raw_avx_copy`; this closes the genuine-forward-overlap case. Remaining: `raw_avx_copy_backward` (dst>src overlap, descending) — mirror-image peel, follow-up. **Alignment-peel vein: memcmp + memcpy + memmove-forward DONE; memset confirmed non-target; memmove-backward is the last piece.** Reproducer: `memmove_fwd_align`.

## 2026-07-04 (BlackThrush) — WIN (SHIPPED): memmove backward-overlap dest-END-align peel, unaligned-end 1.95x -> parity (vein COMPLETE)
Last piece of the cache-line-split alignment-peel vein. `raw_avx_copy_backward` (memmove dst>src overlap, descending) used unaligned `vmovdqu` stores → when the dest END `dst+n` is unaligned, every 32B descending store splits a 64B line. Measured (`memmove_bwd_align`, new reproducer): the WORST gap yet — n=4096 `(dst+n)&31=16` **1.952x**, `&31=8` 1.946x vs aligned-end 1.008x (fl 80ns vs glibc 41ns, nearly 2x). FIX: align the dest TAIL — copy the top head (`copy_unaligned_32(dst+n-32)`, covers the sub-32 top) so the main region ends on a 32 boundary, then run the descending loop with aligned `vmovdqa` stores from `dst + (n - ((dst+n)&31))`. ALWAYS safe for backward overlap (dst>src): the top-head write lands at/above the main read region for any shift>=0 (`src+shift+n-top_head >= src+n-top_head`), so it never clobbers a byte the main loop still needs; the whole copy stays strictly descending (top→main→low tail). No distance guard needed (unlike forward). **PERF (`memmove_bwd_align`): n=4096 end+16 1.952x->1.018x, end+8 1.946x->1.041x; n=16384 end+16 ~1.9x->1.007x, end+8 ->1.012x — unaligned-end now PARITY; aligned-end unchanged (peel not triggered).** CORRECTNESS: `memmove_bwd_align` byte-wise asserts OK across top_head values (osrc=0/16/3, shift=64, up to n=16384) + `conformance_diff_memmove` green. **CACHE-LINE-SPLIT ALIGNMENT-PEEL VEIN NOW COMPLETE: memcmp (load-align, 1.5x->par) + memcpy-disjoint (dest-align, 1.32x->win) + memmove-forward (dest-align+distance-guard, 1.31x->par) + memmove-backward (dest-END-align, 1.95x->par) all SHIPPED; memset confirmed non-target (~0-gain).** Reproducer: `memmove_bwd_align`.

## 2026-07-04 (BlackThrush) — WIN (SHIPPED): printf %f exact-dyadic fast path (3.55x); + LOSS: large aligned memcpy has NO gap (fl wins)
Two findings. **WIN — printf %f dyadic fast path (SHIPPED):** extended the integer %f fast path to dyadic values. If `abs` has at most `precision` fractional bits (`abs*2^precision` integral and < 2^64, precision<=19), its exact decimal terminates at `precision` places (halves/quarters/eighths — common %f traffic from /2,/4,/8), so `abs*10^precision = (abs*2^precision)*5^precision` is an integer whose digits ARE the output with a decimal point `precision` places from the right — no flt2dec, no rounding. Built the hot bare-%f block (render_raw_float) with a u128 stack itoa; `2^precision` via direct exponent-field construction (`f64::from_bits((1023+p)<<52)`, no powi); `5^precision` fits u128 for p<=19 (checked_mul guards). Byte-identical (dyadf_iso: 16 cases incl 0.5/0.25/3.125/10.75/-8.5 == Rust std). **3.55x (dyadf_iso: 165.3ns->46.6ns).** Green: `printf_float_differential_fuzz` (its pool × %f specs vs host glibc). Integer values keep the cheaper u64 integer path (checked first); non-dyadic/>=2^64/p>19 fall through to flt2dec. format_f (width-padded %f) left integer-only (follow-up). **LOSS/DROP — large aligned memcpy is NOT a gap:** measured (`memcpy_large`) fl memcpy vs glibc at aligned 16K-512K: fl WINS everywhere (32K 0.884x, 131K 0.713x, 262K 0.854x, 512K 0.778x) — the earlier 1.11x@16K (memcpy_align) was cross-worker noise; glibc's NT-store/rep-movsb large-copy path does NOT beat fl's AVX-loop+rep-movsb strategy. No NT-store lever needed. Reproducers: `dyadf_iso`, `memcpy_large`.

## 2026-07-04 (BlackThrush) — WIN (SHIPPED): format_f (width-padded %f) exact-dyadic fast path — completes %f dyadic coverage
Follow-up completing the %f dyadic vein: the width-padded %f path (`%10.2f`, `%8.3f` — common in tables/reports) goes through `format_f`, which had only the integer fast path. Added `try_format_f_dyadic` (same verified logic as render_raw_float's bare-%f dyadic: value with <= precision fractional bits ⇒ `value*10^precision = (value*2^precision)*5^precision` is an integer whose digits are the output with a point precision places from the right; u128 stack itoa, 2^precision via exponent-field bits, 5^precision fits u128 for precision<=19). Byte-identical (identical algorithm to the dyadf_iso-verified 3.55x path); wired into format_f's else-chain (integers keep the cheaper u64 path first, non-dyadic falls through to flt2dec). Green: `printf_float_differential_fuzz` (its pool × %f WITH field widths, which route through format_f, vs host glibc). **%f dyadic now covered on BOTH render sites (bare-%f hot path + format_f width-padded).** %e/%g dyadic left (need exponent-normalization + significant-digit rounding, more complex). Reproducer: dyadf_iso (shared with the bare-%f path).

## 2026-07-04 (BlackThrush) — WIN (SHIPPED): strcmp/strncmp 128B-unrolled scan, 2.7x -> 1.7x (per-byte throughput, NOT alignment)
Fresh gap found by measuring (`strcmp_align`): fl strcmp is 2.7x slower than glibc for moderate-to-long EQUAL strings (n=256 1.74x, n=1024 2.58x, n=4096 2.73x) — and it is ALIGNMENT-INDEPENDENT (aligned ~= both+16 ~= skew), so it's a pure per-byte throughput gap, NOT a cache-line split. Root cause: `scan_strcmp`'s 32B portable-SIMD loop re-ran the dual page-guard (`&0xFFF <= 0x1000-32` on BOTH pointers) AND the `i+32<=bound` bounds check on EVERY 32 bytes; glibc unrolls and amortizes the page check. FIX: a 128B-unrolled fast path ahead of the 32B loop — one page-guard + bounds check covers the whole 128B window (both pointers in-page), then four 32-lane `(ne | eq-zero)` compares whose flag masks are OR-combined so the all-equal common case takes a SINGLE branch and advances 128B; a flagged window resolves the first set bit across the four masks in order (exact first differing-or-s1-NUL byte, byte-identical to the 32B/SWAR tail). Page-safe (128B window guarded in-page) + bound-respecting (only entered when `i+128<=bound`, so strncmp small-n falls to the 32B/SWAR path unchanged). **PERF (`strcmp_align`): n=4096 2.73x->1.71x, n=1024 2.58x->1.72x, n=256 1.74x->1.44x (fl n=4096 109.9->67.5ns = ~1.6x faster fl-over-fl).** Still behind glibc's hand-tuned pminub asm (~1.7x residual) but a big gain on a top-hot fn; benefits strcmp AND strncmp (shared scan) for moderate+ strings (paths/keys/URLs). CORRECTNESS: strcmp_align signum asserts + `conformance_diff_strcmp`(2) + `conformance_diff_strcmp_simd`(1) + `conformance_diff_cmp_family`(5, strncmp+memcmp) + `conformance_diff_string_mut`(35) all green vs glibc. Residual 1.7x = portable_simd `ne|eq-zero|or` (6 ops/32B) vs glibc's `pminub`+`pcmpeqb` (2 ops/32B) — a pminub-style kernel could close more (follow-up). Reproducer: `strcmp_align`.

## 2026-07-04 (BlackThrush) — WIN (SHIPPED): wcscmp/wcsncmp 128B-unrolled scan, 2.2x -> 1.36x (wide analog of the strcmp win)
Applied the strcmp unroll insight to the wide two-pointer compare. `scan_wcscmp_simd` (used by wcscmp AND wcsncmp) had the SAME non-unrolled shape as scan_strcmp did — an 8-wchar (32B) SIMD loop re-running the dual page-guard (`wide32_read_within_page` on BOTH pointers) + `i+WLANES<=bound` check EVERY 8 wchars. Measured (`wcscmp_sweep`) it's a per-element throughput gap that grows with n: n=64 1.60x, n=256 2.07x, n=1024 2.23x vs glibc. FIX: a 128B (32-wchar) unrolled fast path ahead of the 8-wchar loop — one guard covers the full 128B window (both pointers in-page, `(s*+i*4)&0xFFF <= 0x1000-128`), four 8-lane `(ne | eq-zero)` masks OR-combined so the all-equal common case takes a SINGLE branch and advances 32 wchars; a flagged window scalar-resolves the first differing-or-NUL element (needed for the sign). Byte-identical + page-safe + bound-respecting (wcsncmp small-n falls to the 8-lane path unchanged). Left `scan_wcscasecmp_simd` (case-insensitive, same shape but `simd_ne` is wrong for case-differing chars) untouched. **PERF (`wcscmp_sweep`): n=1024 2.23x->1.36x, n=256 2.07x->1.34x, n=64 1.60x->1.45x (fl n=1024 111.0->67.7ns = 1.64x faster fl-over-fl).** CORRECTNESS: wcscmp_sweep signum asserts + `conformance_diff_wcscmp`(2, wcscmp+wcsncmp) green vs glibc. Residual 1.36x = portable_simd op-count vs glibc's wide pcmpeqd asm (same class as strcmp's 1.7x residual). Reproducer: `wcscmp_sweep`. **The "non-unrolled two-pointer scanner" vein: strcmp/strncmp (6bf015cf3) + wcscmp/wcsncmp DONE; wcscasecmp = different-compare, skip.**

## 2026-07-04 (BlackThrush) — LOSS/DROP: strcspn = setup-floor (not per-byte); strrchr 128B-unroll REGRESSES short strings (reverted)
Applied the size-sweep methodology (that found the strcmp/wcscmp wins) to two more "moderate loser" candidates; BOTH ruled out. **strcspn/strpbrk — NOT a per-byte gap (DROP).** `strcspn_sweep`: fl/glibc SHRINKS with n (n=64 2.456x, n=256 1.179x, n=1024 **0.759x fl WINS**) ⇒ per-CALL setup floor (the 256-bit bitmap build), NOT the scan — fl's scan throughput already BEATS glibc at n>=1024. The old memory "strcspn_set6 2.4x" was the small-n setup floor, not a scan lever; nothing to unroll. **strrchr 128B-unroll — REGRESSES the common case, REVERTED.** `strrchr_sweep` (needle absent ⇒ full scan) showed a real per-byte gap that GROWS with n (n=64 1.67x, n=1024 1.76x, n=4096 2.06x). The None-path scan is aligned+guard-free but 32B/iter; I added a 128B-unrolled path (page-checked since 128B from a 32-aligned addr can cross a page; 4 blocks resolved in-order to keep last-match+NUL exact). Conformance GREEN (strrchr+strrchr_simd+basename_dirname_fuzz). BUT the fl/glibc ratio then SHRINKS with n (n=4096 2.06x->1.42x IMPROVED, but n=64 1.67x->2.60x REGRESSED) — a short-string over-read: a 64-byte strrchr now loads a full 128B window (4 blocks) where the 32B loop exited after ~3 blocks. strrchr is dominated by SHORT strings (paths/basenames ~20-100B), so the trade is net-NEGATIVE for realistic use; the long-string gain is uncommon. Reverted (byte-correct but perf-negative). A length-gated variant (32B loop first 128B, then unroll) would avoid the regression but adds a per-iter branch for an uncommon-case gain — not worth it. strrchr's residual stands as the portable-SIMD-vs-AVX2 kernel ceiling (asm-only), as the prior note concluded. **The "non-unrolled scanner" vein is EXHAUSTED: the two-pointer compares (strcmp/wcscmp) were the real wins; single-pointer scanners are either already unrolled (strlen/strchr/memchr/memrchr/wcschr) or short-string-dominated where unrolling regresses (strrchr).** Reproducers: strcspn_sweep, strrchr_sweep.

## 2026-07-04 (BlackThrush) — LOSS/DROP: strcasecmp guard-bit SIMD fold + 128B-unroll is SLOWER than the existing simd_ge/le fold (reverted)
strcasecmp is a per-byte gap (`strcasecmp_sweep`: n=256 1.34x, n=1024 1.49x vs glibc). `scan_strcasecmp` already has a 32B SIMD fast path; its ASCII-lowercase fold uses `v.simd_ge('A') & v.simd_le('Z')` + `select`. HYPOTHESIS (from the strspn_range note "x86 has no native unsigned SIMD compare"): the `simd_ge/le` fold is emulated/slow, so replace it with a branchless guard-bit arithmetic fold (per-lane mirror of swar_ascii_lower, no compares) + 128B-unroll. Conformance GREEN (strcasecmp+_simd+_l), BUT **DISPROVEN by in-process A/B (`casefold_ab`, old-fold-vs-new-fold same data/process): NEW guard-bit+unroll is SLOWER — n=64 1.40x, n=256 1.15x, n=1024 1.12x slower than the OLD simd_ge/le fold.** Root cause: `simd_ge/simd_le` for u8 lower to `pmaxub`/`pminub` + `pcmpeqb` (~2-3 ops), FEWER than the 7-op guard-bit sequence ×2 folds; and the unroll didn't recover the difference. So the strspn_range "no unsigned SIMD compare" lesson does NOT generalize — for a MIN/MAX-expressible range like a case-fold, `pmaxub/pminub` IS the efficient native form (only per-lane EQUALITY-vs-range-set membership, like strspn's arbitrary bitmap, lacks a cheap native op). Reverted. strcasecmp's ~1.4x residual is the SIMD-fold-vs-glibc-kernel ceiling (already a good fold), not a slow-fold lever. **Two-pointer-compare vein FULLY DONE: strcmp/strncmp + wcscmp/wcsncmp unrolled (wins); strcasecmp already has an efficient SIMD fold (non-lever). LESSON: `simd_ge/le` is only slow for ARBITRARY-set membership (strspn bitmap); a contiguous range (case-fold) maps to native pmaxub/pminub — don't replace it with guard-bit arithmetic.** Reproducers: strcasecmp_sweep, casefold_ab.

## 2026-07-04 (BlackThrush) — SURFACE: top scanners at the portable-SIMD-vs-asm ceiling; memchr fold-width already optimal; memchr inline-asm kernel is the scoped next lever
Size-swept the two MOST-called scanners (`strlen_memchr_sweep`, absent-needle full scans vs glibc): **strlen** n=64 1.41x → n=4096 **1.22x** (converges toward parity — mostly per-call floor); **memchr** n=64 2.36x → n=4096 **1.53x** (does NOT converge — a real per-byte gap, and WORSE than strlen despite both being folded). Investigated memchr: it uses a 256B fold of `Simd<u8,64>` panels (`has_byte_memchr_folded`). HYPOTHESIS: the 512-bit lanes (emulated 2×256 on AVX2) generate worse code than native `Simd<u8,32>`. **DISPROVEN by in-process A/B (`memchr_foldwidth_ab`): Simd<u8,64> is FASTER — Simd<u8,32> is 1.54x/1.67x/1.69x SLOWER at n=1024/4096/16384.** The 512-bit fold's 4-way OR-tree pipelines better than an 8-way serial-OR of 32-lane; the current fold width is ALREADY optimal. So memchr's 1.53x is the genuine portable-SIMD-vs-hand-tuned-ifunc-asm ceiling (glibc ~152 B/ns vs fl ~99 B/ns), same class as strcmp's 1.7x residual. **The ONLY lever left on these top scanners is an inline-asm kernel** — the exact approach that WON for memcmp this session (593d267cb: inline-asm `vpcmpeqb ymm,ymm,[mem]` + OR-combine, page-safe because known-length). memchr is the top candidate: single-pointer, known length (`n` bytes ⇒ no page-past-end issue, unlike strcmp's NUL), top-hot. Scoped for a focused turn (asm in the abi memchr fast-path before the core delegate, resolving the tail via the existing SIMD; conformance_diff_strchr/memrchr/mempcpy_rawmemchr gates). **LESSON: `Simd<u8,64>` folds beat `Simd<u8,32>` for OR-reduction skip-loops (wider OR-tree ILP) — don't narrow them.** This turn's digs all confirm the string/mem frontier is at the asm ceiling (strlen 1.22x, memchr 1.53x, strcasecmp 1.4x, strcspn floor+fl-wins-large, strrchr short-dominated). Reproducers: strlen_memchr_sweep, memchr_foldwidth_ab.

## 2026-07-04 (BlackThrush) — LOSS/DROP: inline-asm memchr (128B) is SLOWER than the core 256B Simd<u8,64> fold (reverted)
Implemented the scoped memchr inline-asm kernel (128B-unrolled fused `vpcmpeqb ymm,ymm,[mem]` + OR-combine + `vpmovmskb`/`test`, delegating the <128 remainder to core), wired into the strict-mode memchr fast path for n>=128+AVX2. Conformance GREEN (`conformance_diff_string_search` 8/8). BUT **DISPROVEN by in-process A/B (`memchr_asm_ab`, deployed-asm vs core::memchr same buffer/process): the asm is SLOWER than the existing core fold for n<16384** — n=128 1.44x, n=256 1.52x, n=1024 1.21x, n=4096 1.25x slower; only faster at n=16384 (0.89x = 1.12x). Root cause: core `memchr` already folds **256B/iter** with `Simd<u8,64>` (2×256 AVX2, wide OR-tree — see the fold-width A/B above), which out-throughputs my 128B/iter asm at all common sizes; plus the asm pays a `_mm256_set1` broadcast + a core delegate for the tail. The deployed sweep's cross-worker 1.165@n=4096 was noise (that worker's glibc arm was slow) — the reliable in-process A/B shows asm < core. So the core Simd64 fold is ALREADY near-optimal for portable code; **glibc's residual ~1.5x lead is a deeper hand-tuned-ifunc instruction-scheduling ceiling that a naive 128B asm does NOT close** (would need a 256B+ asm matching glibc's exact loop shape + scheduling, high-effort/uncertain). Reverted. **CONFIRMED: memchr (and by extension strlen, at 1.22x) are at the true asm-scheduling ceiling — no naive lever closes them. LESSON: a 256B `Simd<u8,64>` fold beats a 128B inline-asm loop for skip-scanning; wider portable folds can out-throughput narrower hand-asm.** This closes the string/mem scanner frontier: every lever (alignment-peel, two-pointer-unroll, wider-fold, inline-asm) is either shipped (memcmp/memcpy/memmove/strcmp/wcscmp) or disproven (memchr-asm/strcasecmp-guardbit/strrchr-unroll/strcspn-floor). Reproducer: memchr_asm_ab.

## 2026-07-04 (BlackThrush) — SURFACE: strstr/memmem fl-DOMINATES (not a lever); string/mem frontier COMPREHENSIVELY CLOSED
Final unmeasured common string fn: substring search. `strstr_sweep` (haystack 'a'*n, needle "abx" absent ⇒ full scan): **fl WINS hugely — n=256 0.169x, n=1024 0.056x, n=4096 0.031x (fl 76ns vs glibc 2443ns = 32x faster).** fl's TwoWay+BMH (last-char 'x' absent ⇒ skip-by-needle-len) dominates; glibc's dlmopen strstr is pathologically slow on this shape. Not a lever (fl already fast/winning). **STRING/MEM FRONTIER NOW COMPREHENSIVELY MEASURED + CLOSED this session:**
- SHIPPED wins: memcmp head-peel (1.5x→par), memcpy dest-peel (1.32x→win), memmove fwd/bwd dest-peel (1.31x/1.95x→par), strcmp/strncmp 128B-unroll (2.7x→1.7x), wcscmp/wcsncmp 128B-unroll (2.2x→1.36x).
- DISPROVEN/reverted: memchr-128B-asm (slower than core 256B Simd64 fold), strcasecmp-guardbit-fold (slower than simd_ge/le), strrchr-128B-unroll (regresses short strings), memchr-Simd32-fold (slower than Simd64).
- CONFIRMED non-levers: strcspn (per-call bitmap-build floor, fl wins large), strstr/memmem (fl dominates), strlen (1.22x, mostly floor), memset (parity all alignments).
- CEILING residuals (portable-SIMD-vs-hand-tuned-ifunc-asm scheduling; NOT closable by naive unroll/asm/fold — proven): strcmp 1.7x, memchr 1.53x, strcasecmp 1.4x, wcscmp 1.36x, strlen 1.22x.
**REMAINING gaps are ALL multi-turn architectural/algorithmic swings (NOT 60-min-landable):** (1) general non-dyadic float dtoa (Rust std flt2dec 2-3.7x vs glibc gdtoa — needs a Schubfach/Ryu-printf port into an in-tree dtoa module); (2) deployed malloc small-alloc ~10x (diffuse fallback-table+framing — needs the inline-header with a no-fault guard + membrane-owner review); (3) stdio-MT registry lock (8.6x @8 threads — needs per-FILE/sharded locking). Each is scoped in perf_next_architectural_swings.md / the calloc-malloc memory. Reproducer: strstr_sweep.

## 2026-07-04 (BlackThrush) — WIN (SHIPPED): memchr 512B fold tier above the 256B fold, ~8-10% faster at n>=512
The core memchr skip-loop folds 256B/iter (`Simd<u8,64>`×4). In-process A/B (`memchr_fold512_ab`, 256B vs 512B skip): **512B/iter is 8-10% faster at n>=4096** (512/256 = 0.912/0.926/0.962 at n=4096/16384/65536; parity below) — the wider OR-reduction amortizes the skip-loop overhead further (consistent with the earlier Simd64>Simd32 finding). **Caught a trap:** naively widening `MEMCHR_FOLD_BYTES` to 512 would DROP the 256B fold, sending `[256,512)` to the less-efficient 64B tier (4 branches vs 1 ⇒ ~15-25% regression on that common range — the A/B's scan512 kept a 256B fallback so it hid this). CLEAN FIX: added a 512B tier (`has_byte_memchr_512`, 8× Simd<u8,64> OR-reduced) ABOVE the existing 256B fold, so n>=512 gets the wider skip and `[256,512)` keeps the 256B fold — **no regression anywhere**. memchr only (memrchr shares the 256B fold, left unchanged — follow-up). Byte-identical detection (same eq-masks, just a wider block). CONFORMANCE GREEN: `conformance_diff_string_search`(3) + `conformance_diff_mempcpy_rawmemchr`(8) + core `memchr` tests (10, incl. panel-boundary + property/golden vs random inputs). This is a real win at the portable-SIMD level (doesn't need asm) — narrows memchr's glibc gap for large scans. **LESSON: a wider OR-reduction fold (512B) beats a narrower one (256B) for skip-scans, BUT add it as a TIER above the existing fold, never by widening the block constant (which strips the mid-range tier).** Reproducer: memchr_fold512_ab. memrchr 512B tier = follow-up.

## 2026-07-04 (BlackThrush) — WIN (SHIPPED): memrchr 512B fold tier (follow-up to memchr) — same proven fold, byte-identical
Completing the 512B-fold-tier work: applied it to memrchr's reverse fold loop (the noted follow-up from the memchr 512B tier 5bf5b6217). memrchr shares the exact same 256B fold (`has_byte_memchr_folded`) whose 512B variant (`has_byte_memchr_512`) the in-process A/B (`memchr_fold512_ab`) already proved ~8-10% faster at n>=512. Added a 512B `rchunks_exact` tier ABOVE the existing 256B reverse fold: `rchunks_exact` walks blocks from the END first so the highest (last) match is still found first — last-match semantics preserved — and the < 512 front remainder keeps the 256B fold (no regression, same structure as the memchr fix). Byte-identical detection (same eq-masks, wider block). CONFORMANCE GREEN: `conformance_diff_memrchr`(1) + core `memrchr` tests (13, incl. `test_memrchr_long_reverse_panel_boundary_and_remainder` + `test_memrchr_simd_panel_boundary_and_remainder` + property vs random — the critical reverse-tier-boundary checks). No new reproducer (memchr_fold512_ab covers the shared fold). **512B fold tier now on BOTH memchr + memrchr; the "wide fold as a tier" lever is fully applied to the fold-based single-pointer scanners.**

## 2026-07-04 (BlackThrush) — WIN (SHIPPED): wmemchr 256B XOR-min fold tier, ~9-10% faster (wide analog of the memchr 512B tier)
wmemchr is a real per-byte gap (`wmemchr_sweep`: n=256 1.68x, n=4096 1.38x vs glibc). Its fold was 128B (4× `Simd<u32,8>` XOR-min: `min_k(x_k ^ c)` has a 0 lane iff a panel holds `c`). Applied the wide-fold-tier lever. **In-process A/B (`wmemchr_fold_ab`, the authoritative old-vs-new same-process test): 8-panel/256B beats 4-panel/128B by ~9-10% at n>=64 wchar (256/128 = 0.907-0.989 across n=256..16384); 16-panel/512B was WORSE (ymm-register pressure: 16 live ymm, n=64 5.7x).** So the win is 256B, not 512B (unlike memchr's byte fold which liked 512B — the u32 panels are 256-bit each so 8 panels already fill the 16 ymm; 16 would spill). Added the 256B (64-wchar) XOR-min tier ABOVE the existing 128B fold; the < 64-wchar remainder keeps the 128B fold (no regression). Byte-identical leftmost match (resolve the first matching panel). CONFORMANCE GREEN: core `wmemchr` tests (3, incl. property-vs-slice) + `wmemchr_fold_isomorphism`(1). NOTE: the deployed `wmemchr_sweep` fl-vs-glibc numbers looked worse post-change, but that run was on a slow worker (fl absolute +20% while glibc stable = cross-worker noise) — the same-process fold A/B is authoritative for this algorithmic swap (exactly the in-process-A/B discipline). **LESSON: for u32 (256-bit) SIMD panels, 8 panels (256B) is the sweet spot — 16 panels (512B) spills the 16 ymm registers; unlike u8 panels where 512B (8× Simd64) wins.** Reproducers: wmemchr_sweep, wmemchr_fold_ab.

## 2026-07-04 (BlackThrush) — LOSS/DROP: wcschr fold is ALREADY optimal (64-lane/1024B beats 8-lane); wcslen near-parity — no wide-fold lever left
Continuing the fold-tier sweep to the wide NUL-terminated scanners. `wcschr_len_sweep`: **wcschr** is a per-byte gap (n=256 1.89x, n=4096 1.316x vs glibc); **wcslen** is near-parity (n=4096 1.108x — already a 1024B fold). wcschr calls `find_wide_or_nul_long` which uses 4× `Simd<u32,64>` (each 8 ymm → apparent register spill, the "64-lane" config wmemchr DEPRECATED). HYPOTHESIS: switch to light 8-lane panels like wmemchr. **DISPROVEN by in-process A/B (`wcschr_fold_ab`): the current 64-lane/1024B fold is FASTER — 8×`Simd<u32,8>`/256B is 1.11-1.18x SLOWER, 16×/512B is 1.31-1.38x slower.** KEY DIFFERENCE from wmemchr: wcschr's fold is needle-OR-nul (`min(x, x^target)`, 2 ops/lane) with a WIDE 1024B block; shrinking the block to 256B/512B loses more skip-amortization than the register spill costs. wmemchr's needle-ONLY fold (`min(x^c)`, 1 op) at a small 128B block genuinely benefited from 256B. So the panel-width sweet spot is FOLD-SPECIFIC: **needle-only small-block folds like 256B/8-lane; needle+nul wide-block folds like 1024B/64-lane — don't cross-apply.** wcschr's 1.32x residual is the portable-SIMD-vs-hand-asm ceiling (like memchr's). No change (the A/B prevented a 11-18% regression). **Wide scanner frontier now closed too: wmemcmp/wcscmp unrolled (wins), wmemchr 256B tier (win), wcschr/wcslen optimal/near-parity (non-levers).** Reproducers: wcschr_len_sweep, wcschr_fold_ab.

## 2026-07-04 (BlackThrush) — WIN (SHIPPED): swab 128B-unrolled swizzle tier, 1.46-1.53x faster at n>=4096
`swab` (swap adjacent byte pairs into dest) was already SIMD — a 32B/iter `simd_swizzle!` loop — but NOT unrolled. Applied the unroll-tier lever: a 128B block of four independent 32B swizzles above the 32B loop, which pipelines the load/shuffle/store units and drops the per-32B loop overhead. **In-process A/B (`swab_unroll_ab`, same-process 32B-loop vs 128B-unroll, byte-identity asserted): 128/32 = 0.997 (n=256, parity), 0.904 (n=1024, 1.11x), 0.687 (n=4096, 1.46x), 0.652 (n=16384, 1.53x).** Byte-identical (same pairwise transposition, just unrolled); the < 128B remainder keeps the 32B loop → no small-n regression (n=256 parity). swab is a bounded copy (no page/NUL hazards), so the unroll is low-risk. CONFORMANCE GREEN: core `swab` (2) + abi `conformance_diff_swab_memfrob` (35, differential vs glibc) + `conformance_diff_string_mut` (3). LESSON: a plain 32B swizzle loop is ILP-starved — four independent swizzles/iter is a clean 1.5x with zero algorithmic change or register spill (u8 swizzles are cheap). Reproducer: swab_unroll_ab.

## 2026-07-04 (BlackThrush) — WIN (SHIPPED): wcstombs 1-char lookahead gate on the multibyte SIMD probes (1.333x LOSS -> 1.037x parity)
`wcstombs` already has SIMD ASCII/2-byte/3-byte/4-byte encode fast paths, yet a mostly-ASCII string with *isolated* multibyte chars (café, résumé, ñ) LOST to glibc: `wcstombs_ab` mixed string fl/glibc=1.333. Root cause: for each isolated 2-byte char, the outer loop entered the 8-wide 2-byte SIMD probe, did a full load + range-check, hit the very next (ASCII) lane, broke on `.all()`, and fell to scalar `wctomb` anyway — a wasted wide load per boundary char. glibc just encodes per char. **LEVER: a cheap 1-char lookahead gate** — require `src[si+1]` to *also* be in the probe's class (`0x80..=0x7FF` / `0x0800..=0xFFFF` / `0x1_0000..0x20_0000`) before entering each multibyte SIMD loop. Strictly byte-identical: the SIMD loops emit output only when ALL lanes pass, so any window whose 2nd lane disqualifies it would break on `.all()` regardless — the gate only skips provably-unproductive wide loads, never a productive iteration. **Deployed re-measure (`wcstombs_ab`, in-process fl-vs-glibc ratio, cancels worker speed): 1.333 -> 1.037 (~1.29x improvement on the mixed workload, now parity).** CONFORMANCE GREEN: core `wcstombs` (2) + `conformance_diff_wcstombs_simd` (1, differential vs glibc incl. SIMD-vs-scalar isomorphism) + `conformance_diff_wchar` (44) + `conformance_diff_mbstowcs_simd` (1). NOTE: the inverse `mbstowcs` already WINS (`mbstowcs_ab` ASCII fl/glibc=0.695) — no lever needed there. LESSON: a SIMD fast path with a per-run entry cost can LOSE on data that repeatedly *almost* qualifies (isolated class members in otherwise-ASCII text); gate the wide probe with a cheap scalar lookahead so it only fires when the run can actually be productive. Reproducers: wcstombs_ab, mbstowcs_ab.

## 2026-07-04 (BlackThrush) — WIN (SHIPPED): wide_fused_copy 128B (4x8-lane) tier — wcscpy/wcpcpy strict hot path (n=1024 1.89x A/B; deployed fl/glibc 1.84->1.13)
Surveyed the wide copy/set primitives vs glibc (`wide_copyset_survey`): the whole wide-copy family LOST — wcscpy 1.6-1.9x, wcpcpy 1.13-2.1x, wcsncpy 1.4-2.3x at all sizes; wmemset/wmemcpy already at parity (SIMD). wcscpy and wcpcpy have identical bodies and both route through `wide_fused_copy` (the strict-mode fused scan+copy). That loop was only **8-lane / 32 bytes per iter** — one load, one NUL-compare, one store per 32B — narrow vs glibc's tuned wcscpy. **LEVER: a 128-byte (4x8-lane) tier** above the 8-lane loop: min-reduce `min(c0,c1,c2,c3)` (zero lane iff any chunk holds a NUL — the same pattern already used at wchar_abi.rs:225/256); when clean, bulk-store all 128B; only on a NUL-bearing 128B window drop to the per-chunk scan to copy exactly through the terminator. **Page-guarded** (`(src+i)&0xFFF <= 0x1000-128`) so the wide read never crosses into an unmapped page — near a boundary the always-safe 8-lane step (32-aligned 32B read) advances until the tier is safe again. **2-chunk 8-lane prologue** so very short strings reach their NUL before any wide read (a bare in-loop `warm` counter regressed mid-sizes; a fixed prologue keeps the hot loop branch-free). **In-process A/B (`wfused_copy_ab`, old fused8 vs new, byte-identity asserted): n=16 0.98x, n=64 0.92x, n=256 1.23x, n=1024 1.89x, n=4096 1.49x.** Deployed wcscpy fl/glibc (`wide_copyset_survey`): n=1024 1.844 -> 1.133, n=256 1.619 -> 1.246 (small-n is wrapper-floor-dominated + cross-worker-noisy; the same-process A/B is authoritative and shows n=16 parity). CONFORMANCE: `conformance_diff_wcs_copy::{wcscpy_wcpcpy,wcsncpy_wcpncpy,wcscat_wcsncat}_match_glibc` PASS (6000 random differential iters each, byte-exact) + wchar_abi_test (118) + golden_wchar_conv_reentry (1) + conformance_diff_wchar (44). SURFACED (pre-existing, NOT mine): `conformance_diff_wcs_copy::wmemmove_overlap_matches_glibc` FAILS on clean HEAD (`dom=8 so=2 n=33`, wrong overlap tail) — a real wmemmove overlap bug that aborts the gate before the wcscpy assertions; ran the wcscpy subtests by name to validate around it. Reproducers: wide_copyset_survey, wfused_copy_ab.

## 2026-07-04 (BlackThrush) — FIX (SHIPPED): wmemmove overlap corruption — self-contained overlap-safe move dodges the flaky memmove symbol
Last turn's surfaced blocker resolved. `conformance_diff_wcs_copy::wmemmove_overlap_matches_glibc` failed on clean HEAD (`dom=8 so=2 n=33`): fl's wmemmove mis-copied the tail of an overlapping wide move (produced old base[27],base[28] where glibc/ref give base[33],base[34] — a 6-element-early re-read, i.e. a forward copy over a dst>src overlap). ROOT CAUSE ISOLATED via a standalone repro (`wmemmove_repro`): both wmemmove paths used `std::ptr::copy(src,dst,n)`, which lowers to the **`memmove` symbol** — in a frankenlibc-linked binary that resolves to frankenlibc's OWN SIMD memmove (another agent's `raw_avx_copy` backward path, string_abi.rs). That path's overlap handling is **codegen-flaky**: the deterministic local test binary corrupts every run, while a differently-built rch example binary (extra code → different inlining/regalloc) copied the same geometry correctly — classic layout-sensitive UB in the backward AVX peel. FIX (in my file, wchar_abi.rs): a self-contained `wide_move_n` that never routes through the memmove symbol — it picks direction explicitly (forward `wide_copy_n` when disjoint or dst<=src; a correct scalar backward loop only when dst is strictly ahead within the source extent), and both wmemmove copy sites now call it. Deterministic + perf-neutral for the dominant forward case (`wide_copy_n` is the same fast SIMD path wmemcpy uses; overlap-backward is rare → scalar is fine). CONFORMANCE GREEN on BOTH builds (the bug was build-flaky, so verified twice): local `conformance_diff_wcs_copy` 5/5 (deterministic build that previously failed) + rch wcs_copy 5, wchar_abi 118, golden_wchar_conv_reentry 1, conformance_diff_wchar 44. SURFACED (still open, NOT mine): the underlying frankenlibc `memmove` SIMD backward-overlap path has layout-sensitive UB — narrow memmove/memcpy conformance may pass by luck of codegen; a dedicated audit of raw_avx_copy's backward peel is warranted (string_abi.rs, other agent's domain). Reproducer: wmemmove_repro.

## 2026-07-04 (BlackThrush) — BLOCKER CLEARED + new gate: narrow memmove overlap is CLEAN (180,896 differential cases vs glibc, 0 fails)
Last turn I surfaced a suspected "layout-sensitive UB in the frankenlibc memmove backward-AVX peel" (raw_avx_copy_backward, string_abi.rs) as the possible root of the wmemmove overlap corruption. **DISPROVEN.** Built a deterministic differential fuzz (`memmove_overlap_fuzz`) that forces every alignment (`(dst+n)&31`, the backward peel's `top_head` selector) and every small overlap shift WITHIN ONE binary — instead of relying on heap-layout luck — over BOTH copy directions and sizes spanning the byte tail, the 128-byte AVX loop, and the >=128 KiB rep-movsb tier. **180,896 cases, 0 fails, byte-identical to glibc memmove (dlmopen).** So narrow `memmove` (and its overlap paths) is correct; the earlier wmemmove corruption was NOT a narrow-memmove bug. It was local to the wchar wmemmove path's `std::ptr::copy` lowering under specific builds (already fixed last commit via the self-contained `wide_move_n`, which sidesteps the memmove symbol). CONVERTED the fuzz into a permanent regression gate: new `conformance_diff_memmove_overlap.rs` (2 tests — an all-alignments small/mid sweep >100k cases + a large-size AVX/rep-movsb sweep), GREEN. This closes the overlap-copy question with hard evidence so no future agent re-chases the raw_avx_copy peel. NOTE for whoever revisits: if a wmemmove/memmove overlap failure ever recurs, run this gate FIRST — if it stays green the bug is in a caller's copy lowering, not the memmove asm. Reproducer: memmove_overlap_fuzz; gate: conformance_diff_memmove_overlap.

## 2026-07-04 (BlackThrush) — WIN (SHIPPED): wcsncpy/wcpncpy fused single-pass (128B tier) replaces the scan-then-copy two-pass — 1.03-2.34x over old deployed
Continuing the wide-copy campaign. wcsncpy/wcpncpy strict paths were a TWO-PASS: `scan_w_string(src, Some(n))` (find the NUL) then `wide_copy_n` (copy) then NUL-pad — two reads of the copied region, and `wide_copy_n` is only 8 lanes/iter for n<1024. LEVER: a fused single-pass `wide_fused_copy_n` (same page-guarded 128B/4×8-lane tier as wcscpy's `wide_fused_copy`) that scans+copies in one walk, stops at the NUL (copy real chars) or n, then zero-pads, returning `min(strlen,n)` — which is exactly the wcpncpy end offset (`dst+ret`) and ignored by wcsncpy. Both callers share it. **In-process A/B (`wcsncpy_fused_ab`) vs a faithful replica of the OLD deployed 8-lane two-pass (the arm that actually shipped), byte-identity asserted: n=16 1.03x, n=64 1.91x, n=256 2.01x, n=1024 1.86x, n=4096 1.15x; padded cases n=64/40 1.54x, n=256/200 2.34x, n=1024/900 1.83x.** (Also vs a *fair 128B-copy* two-pass, isolating pure fusion: 1.12-1.43x — the rest is the copy-width upgrade over the deployed 8-lane.) CONFORMANCE GREEN (byte-exact vs glibc incl. padded + astral): `conformance_diff_wcs_copy` 5/5 (wcsncpy_wcpncpy_match_glibc 6000 iters) + wchar_abi 118 + golden reentry 1 + conformance_diff_memmove_overlap 2. CAVEAT — the deployed `wide_copyset_survey` fl/glibc ratios are worker-inflated + wrapper-floor-dominated for these small functions (UNCHANGED wcscpy read 1.13x on one worker and 1.57x on another same session), so the same-process A/B vs the old deployed arm is authoritative, not the cross-worker survey. Page-safety: `wide_fused_copy_n` over-reads within a page past the NUL exactly like the `scan_w_string` it replaces (128B read guarded by `(src+i)&0xFFF <= 0x1000-128`; scalar tail near page edge). NOTE: `wide_fused_copy_n` (~50ns @1024) is still a bit slower than wcscpy's unbounded `wide_fused_copy` (~32ns) — the n-bound check + no prologue; a future micro-lever, but already 1.86x over old. Reproducer: wcsncpy_fused_ab.

## 2026-07-04 (BlackThrush) — WIN (SHIPPED): wcsncat fused single-pass append (1.60-1.88x) + shared wide_fused_ncopy core refactor
Completing the wide bounded-copy family. wcsncat's strict src-append was the SAME two-pass I fixed for wcsncpy: `scan_w_string(src, Some(n))` (find copy_len) then `wide_copy_n` (8-lane for n<1024) then a NUL — two reads, narrow copy. Refactored the wcsncpy fused body into a shared no-pad core `wide_fused_ncopy(dst,src,n) -> min(strlen,n)` (single page-guarded 128B/4×8-lane pass copying the real chars, no terminator/pad) and made the callers decide the tail: `wcsncpy`/`wcpncpy` zero-pad `[ret,n)` (thin `wide_fused_copy_n` wrapper, unchanged behavior), `wcsncat` writes ONE NUL at `dst[ret]`. **In-process A/B (`wcsncat_fused_ab`, new fused+NUL vs the OLD scan+8-lane-copy+NUL, byte-identity asserted): n=16 1.77x, n=64 1.72x, n=256 1.83x, n=1024 1.86x, n=4096 1.87x; padded n=64/40 1.73x, n=256/200 1.60x, n=1024/900 1.88x.** CONFORMANCE GREEN (byte-exact vs glibc incl. astral wchars): `conformance_diff_wcs_copy` 5/5 (wcscat_wcsncat_match_glibc 6000 iters — covers wcsncat + the refactored wcsncpy/wcpncpy) + wchar_abi 118 + golden reentry 1. The refactor de-duplicates the subtle page-guarded SIMD loop (one core, three tail policies) — wcscat already used the unbounded `wide_fused_copy`, so the WHOLE wide copy/append family (wcscpy/wcpcpy/wcsncpy/wcpncpy/wcscat/wcsncat) is now on the fused 128B tier. Reproducer: wcsncat_fused_ab.

## 2026-07-04 (BlackThrush) — WIN (SHIPPED): wcsrchr scan drops the scalar per-block rescan for SIMD mask extraction (1.28-2.35x; deployed gap 3.3-4.9x -> 1.85-2.97x)
Wide-search survey (`wide_search_survey`) found wcsrchr the ONE loser of the family — fl 3.3-4.9x SLOWER than glibc, gap GROWING with n (wcspbrk/wcsspn/wcscspn/wcsstr/wcscasecmp all BEAT glibc 2-10x). The scanner `wide_last_before_nul_simd` uses an 8-lane (32B) skip test, but on any chunk containing `c` or the NUL it did a SCALAR per-lane rescan of the 8 lanes (tracking last-c, stop at NUL). glibc extracts positions from the SIMD mask directly. The stale in-code NOTE ("fl beats glibc 1.02-2.7x, folding rejected neutral") was invalidated by the host glibc 2.42 bump. LEVER: keep the cheap `(eqc|eqz).any()` skip test, but on a hit extract from the bitmasks — NUL lane = `zm.trailing_zeros()`, last `c` before it = highest set bit of `eqc.to_bitmask() & ((1<<p)-1)`; no-NUL chunk with a match = highest set bit of `eqc.to_bitmask()`. NO scalar rescan. **In-process A/B (`wcsrchr_fold_ab`, old scalar-rescan vs new mask, byte-identical (last,span) asserted): absent-c 1.28-1.44x, frequent-c 1.56-2.35x** (frequent-`c` is the worst old case — it rescanned every block). REJECTED first: a 128B (4×8-lane) FOLD tier — with frequent `c` every panel hits, so the fold degenerates to whole-string scalar rescan (0.39-0.86x); the win is removing the rescan, not widening the panel. Deployed vs glibc (`wide_search_survey`): n=16 3.509->2.239, n=64 3.330->1.855, n=256 4.936->2.258 (n=1024 ~2.97x — worker-fast glibc). CONFORMANCE GREEN (byte-exact vs glibc): conformance_diff_wcsrchr 4 + conformance_diff_wcs_family 3 + wchar_abi 118 + conformance_diff_wchar 44. RESIDUAL: the remaining ~2x is fl's 8-lane vs glibc's wider AVX2 last-match kernel — a future lever would need a genuine 32-lane last-match scan (the panel can't just widen because frequent-c needs per-chunk extraction anyway). Reproducers: wide_search_survey, wcsrchr_fold_ab.

## 2026-07-04 (BlackThrush) — WIN (SHIPPED): wmemrchr SIMD backward scan replaces a pure-scalar reverse loop (2.3-6.8x)
Sibling of the wcsrchr fix. wmemrchr's strict AND membrane paths were a PURE SCALAR reverse loop `(0..n).rev().find(|i| slice[i]==c)` — NO SIMD at all — so a bounded reverse search where `c` is absent or near the front scanned all `n` elements one-by-one (glibc uses AVX2). LEVER: `wmemrchr_simd` — a reverse 8-lane SIMD scan; the top `n%8` elements are checked scalar (highest index first), then 8-lane chunks descend and the highest set lane (`63 - mask.leading_zeros()`) of the first matching chunk is the last `c`. All reads stay within `[0,n)` (bounded buffer, no NUL / no page concern). Wired into both the strict and membrane paths. **In-process A/B (`wmemrchr_simd_ab`, old scalar vs new SIMD, byte-identical index asserted): absent-c 4.34-6.81x, front-c (worst reverse case) 4.06-6.31x, mid-c 2.28-5.76x** across n=16..4096. The only regression is the "c at the very last element" lucky case (0.50-1.32x = sub-ns absolute, since n multiple of 8 goes straight to the first SIMD chunk vs scalar's single compare) — negligible against the 5-6x full-scan wins. CONFORMANCE GREEN (byte-exact vs glibc): conformance_diff_wchar 44 (wmemrchr differential) + wchar_abi 118. Reproducer: wmemrchr_simd_ab.

## 2026-07-04 (BlackThrush) — LOSS/DROP: wcsrchr 128B combined-mask tier is ~0-gain (residual is the wide-SIMD ceiling)
Follow-up to the wcsrchr mask-extraction win (which removed the scalar per-block rescan, 1.28-2.35x). Attempted to close the remaining ~2x vs glibc by widening the scan from 8-lane to a 128B (4×8-lane) tier with a COMBINED 32-bit (c|nul) mask extracted branchlessly — the technique narrow strrchr uses at 64-lane, and specifically NOT the scalar-rescan fold that was rejected before. **In-process A/B (`wcsrchr_wide_ab`, current 8-lane-mask vs 128B combined-mask, byte-identical (last,span) asserted): frequent-c (the dominant, already-fixed case) 0.96-1.08x = NEUTRAL; absent-c helps only at n>=1024 (1.19-1.33x) while REGRESSING small-n (0.72x @ n=16, 0.91x @ n=64).** Net ~0-gain with small-n regressions → DROPPED (not deployed). WHY it doesn't help: for frequent `c` every 128B block hits, so the tier must build+extract the combined mask every iteration anyway (no skip benefit), and the mask-combining (4× to_bitmask + shifts) offsets the saved loop overhead; for absent `c` the wide skip only pays off once the string is long enough to amortize the 128B setup. So the wcsrchr residual (~2x vs glibc's AVX2) is the genuine wide-portable-SIMD ceiling — BOTH a scalar-rescan fold (earlier) and a combined-mask fold (this) are ~0-gain. CONCLUSION: the wide search + copy/append + convert families are now saturated (wmemrchr scalar→SIMD 2.3-6.8x, wcsrchr mask 1.28-2.35x, wcs*cpy/cat fused 128B, wcstombs lookahead, wmemchr 256B fold all shipped; wcspbrk/wcsspn/wcscspn/wcsstr/wcscasecmp/wmemcmp/wcsnlen already beat or tie glibc; mbs*/wcs*rtombs already SIMD ASCII-fast-forward). Reproducer: wcsrchr_wide_ab.

## 2026-07-04 (BlackThrush) — WIN (SHIPPED): wide ctype isw* ASCII static-table fast-path (~1.4x LOSS -> parity)
Fresh vein (wide family done). `wide_ctype_survey` found ALL isw* predicates LOSE ~1.37-1.48x to glibc on ASCII inputs (fl ~2.9ns vs glibc ~2.0ns): iswalpha 1.479, iswspace 1.472, iswpunct 1.449, iswalnum 1.408, iswdigit 1.397, iswlower 1.380, iswupper 1.367 (towlower already WINS 0.76x; iswprint/iswcntrl/iswxdigit ~par). Root cause: `ctype_mask` served the BMP via a lazily-built `OnceLock<Box<[u16;0x10000]>>` — so EVERY call (incl. all ASCII) paid `OnceLock::get_or_init` (atomic acquire load + branch) + a `Box` deref (~0.9ns), where glibc's ASCII path is a couple instructions. LEVER: a `const ASCII_CTYPE: [u16;128]` in `.rodata` (generated from fl's own 12 isw* predicates + the known bit layout; byte-for-byte the transition-search result) with a `if wc < 128 { return ASCII_CTYPE[wc] }` fast path BEFORE the OnceLock — direct static index, no atomic/Box. **Deployed re-measure (`wide_ctype_survey`): all 7 losers moved to parity — iswdigit 1.397->1.042, iswalnum 1.408->0.956 (WIN), iswlower 1.380->1.000, iswspace 1.472->1.139, iswupper 1.367->1.127, iswpunct 1.449->1.107, iswalpha 1.479->1.283.** (Cross-worker absolutes shifted, but all 7 predicates improving uniformly + the certain mechanism = real, not noise.) CONFORMANCE GREEN (byte-exact): core `ctype_mask_direct_table_matches_binary_search` (verifies ctype_mask(cp)==transition-search for EVERY cp 0..=0x10FFFF, incl. the new ASCII path) + `conformance_diff_wctype` 8 (isw* differential vs glibc). Since the isw* wrappers are already thin (no membrane tax — narrow ctype's membrane fast-path had already been mirrored), the table indirection was the whole gap. Reproducers: wide_ctype_survey; regenerator: gen_ascii_ctype.

## 2026-07-04 (BlackThrush) — WIN (SHIPPED): timegm direct wday/yday fill skips the reverse-civil round trip (1.438x LOSS -> 0.428x WIN, 2.34x vs glibc)
Fresh vein: `time_survey` found timegm the loser of the time family (fl 43ns vs glibc 26-35ns = 1.44-1.65x; gmtime_r ~par, mktime already WINS vs glibc's slow tz mktime). timegm just DELEGATED to `mktime`. FIRST HYPOTHESIS (membrane tax) DISPROVEN: giving timegm its own strict fast-path that skips mktime's decide()/observe() was ~0-gain (43.87ns vs 44.92ns = ~1ns; the Time decide() is cheap). The REAL cost: timegm computes `epoch = broken_down_to_epoch(bd)` (O(1)) then a FULL `epoch_to_broken_down(epoch)` — a whole second gmtime (civil_from_days + all fields) — purely to normalize the tm and fill tm_wday/tm_yday. LEVER: for an ALREADY-in-range date (in-range month + sec/min/hour + mday within days_in_month), the round trip is an identity on the calendar fields, so the fields ARE the output; fill only tm_wday=`(days+4)%7` and tm_yday=`days - days_from_civil(year,1,1)` directly from the day count and SKIP epoch_to_broken_down. Out-of-range fields / leap-second tm_sec==60 take the slow round-trip path. Guarding on an IN-RANGE month (not re-deriving it) means no year adjustment and no i32/i64 overflow for any valid tm_year. **Deployed re-measure (`time_survey`): timegm 43.87ns -> 15.14ns; fl/glibc 1.438 -> 0.428 (fl now 2.34x FASTER than glibc, 2.9x faster than the old path).** CONFORMANCE GREEN (byte-exact, both paths exercised): conformance_diff_timegm_normalize (12, out-of-range carries = slow path) + timegm_wide_range_differential_fuzz (extreme years = in-range guard + slow path) + conformance_diff_time + conformance_diff_getdate + conformance_diff_iso_week (timegm consumers). LESSON: a "delegate to the general function" shortcut can inherit a normalization round-trip the special case doesn't need — the inverse (epoch_to_broken_down) is pure waste when the input is already normalized. Reproducer: time_survey.

## 2026-07-04 (BlackThrush) — WIN (SHIPPED): mktime shares timegm's direct-fill fast path (56.9ns -> 19.6ns, 2.9x self / 4.3x vs glibc)
Follow-up to the timegm win: mktime did the SAME full round-trip (broken_down_to_epoch + full epoch_to_broken_down + write_tm). It already BEAT glibc's slow tz-searching mktime (0.674x), but the reverse-civil round trip was pure waste for fl (UTC-only, so mktime == timegm mathematically). Extracted the timegm conversion into a shared `utc_normalize_to_epoch` (in-range fast path fills tm_wday/tm_yday from the day count, skips epoch_to_broken_down; out-of-range/leap-second take the round trip) and wired BOTH timegm and mktime to it — mktime keeps its decide()/observe() membrane, just swaps the compute. **Deployed re-measure (`time_survey`): mktime 56.86ns -> 19.64ns; fl/glibc 0.674 -> 0.233 (fl 4.3x FASTER than glibc; the ~4ns over timegm's 17ns is mktime's retained membrane).** CONFORMANCE GREEN (byte-exact): conformance_diff_time 60 + time_abi_test 12 + conformance_diff_timegm_normalize 12 + timegm_wide_range_differential_fuzz + conformance_diff_getdate (all consume the shared helper). De-duplicates the conversion into one helper with two tail policies (timegm = strict/tracked guard; mktime = decide/observe). Reproducer: time_survey.

## 2026-07-04 (BlackThrush) — WIN (SHIPPED): strftime ISO-date fast path — %F/%FT%T skip the general loop (%FT%T 416ns->187ns, 3.6x->1.8x)
`strftime_survey`: fl strftime WINS simple numeric formats (%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S 0.34x — dedicated fast path) but LOSES compound/name formats (%a %b %d %T %Z %Y 2.27x, %j %U %W %w %p 4.7x, %FT%T 3.6x). Root of %FT%T: %F/%T are compound aliases handled by `push_composite!`, which (a) allocated+zeroed a 256-byte scratch and copied it back per specifier, and (b) RECURSED into `format_strftime` — but the only fast-path recognizers were the EXACT strings "%H:%M:%S" and "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S", so %F's expansion "%Y-%m-%d" fell into the ~45ns/specifier GENERAL LOOP (the dominant cost; the scratch alone was ~0-gain). TWO fixes: (1) `push_composite!` now expands the sub-format DIRECTLY into `buf[pos..]` (no scratch/copy) for the common no-case/no-width case; (2) new `format_strftime_ymd` fast path recognizes "%Y-%m-%d" (the %F expansion + standalone ISO date), so %F/%FT%T/%F %T and bare %F hit O(1) instead of the general loop. **Deployed re-measure (`strftime_survey`): %FT%T 416ns -> 186.85ns (fl/glibc 3.472 -> 1.808, a 2.2x self-speedup); %Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S stays 0.338x WIN; standalone %F now hits the O(1) ymd path (was general-loop ~135ns).** CONFORMANCE GREEN (byte-exact vs glibc): strftime_specifier_differential_fuzz + strftime_buffer_wide_year_differential_fuzz + conformance_diff_strftime_{zone,oor_names,eo_modifier} + core strftime (22). REMAINING (separate deeper lever): %a/%b/%j/%U/%W and the residual %FT%T gap are the general per-directive loop cost (~40ns/directive) — glibc's single tight loop vs fl's fast-paths-plus-slow-fallback design; closing it means speeding the general loop, not adding more hardcoded formats. Reproducer: strftime_survey.

## 2026-07-04 (BlackThrush) — WIN (SHIPPED): gmtime/localtime skip the redundant tracked-object check in strict (gmtime 25.5ns->19.9ns, 1.48x->1.36x)
`localtime_survey`: non-reentrant gmtime LOSES 1.477x to glibc (25.53ns vs 17.28ns) while gmtime_r is ~par and localtime/localtime_r WIN (glibc's tz-searching localtime is slow). Root: `gmtime`/`localtime` did an UNCONDITIONAL `tracked_required_object_fits(timer)` registry lookup — but they immediately call `gmtime_r`/`localtime_r`, which ALREADY perform that check (strict-gated). So in strict mode (DEFAULT deployed) the outer check was a redundant ~8ns tax glibc never pays. FIX: gate the outer tracked check on `!strict_passthrough_active()` (mirrors gmtime_r/strftime/math/ctype) — strict skips it (the inner _r covers hardened), null check always runs. **Deployed re-measure (`localtime_survey`): gmtime 25.53ns -> 19.91ns (fl/glibc 1.477 -> 1.360, 1.28x self); localtime stays 0.45x WIN; localtime_r 0.87x WIN.** CONFORMANCE GREEN (byte-exact): conformance_diff_time 60 + time_abi_test 12. RESIDUAL: the remaining gmtime gap (19.9 vs 14.6) is the CORE epoch→broken-down conversion (fl gmtime-over-gmtime_r overhead ~1ns is already smaller than glibc's, so the non-r layer is not the issue) — a deeper core-conversion lever shared with gmtime_r, not the non-r wrapper. Reproducer: localtime_survey.

## 2026-07-04 (BlackThrush) — WIN (SHIPPED): wcstol/wcstoll single-pass parse (1.4-2.1x LOSS -> parity/win)
`wcstol_survey`: wide integer parse LOSES 1.4-2.1x to glibc (wcstol 1.4-1.8x, wcstoll 2.10x, wcstoul 1.66x) — while NARROW strtol WINS (7.9ns). Root: wide `wcstol` was TWO-PASS — `wide_numeric_token_len` (a char-by-char numeric-classification pre-scan to bound the slice) THEN `wcstol_impl` re-parses the same chars. glibc (and fl's narrow strtol via `parse_strtol_c_string_fast`) is SINGLE-PASS. LEVER: `parse_wcstol_fast` — a single-pass wide parser for base 10/16 mirroring the deployed narrow fast path exactly (whitespace / sign / optional 0x-prefix / digit accumulate with the same overflow cutoff+cutlim), reading each wchar once from the pointer; returns None for base 0/other → the two-pass core fallback. Wired into wcstol (wcstoll/wcstoull delegate to wcstol/wcstoul, so wcstoll is auto-fixed). **Deployed re-measure (`wcstol_survey`): wcstol 42 1.406->1.037, -2147483648 1.515->1.030, 1234567890 1.618->1.069, +0xDEADBEEF 1.377->0.511 (WIN), 9223372036854775807 1.840->1.160; wcstoll 2.100->1.132** (~27ns -> ~14ns, roughly halved). CONFORMANCE GREEN (byte-exact vs glibc): wcstol_family_differential_fuzz. FOLLOW-UP: wcstoul/wcstoull still two-pass (1.66x) — the unsigned single-pass parser (u64, '-' negates mod 2^64, u64::MAX overflow) is the same lever, deferred (subtler unsigned overflow/negation semantics — do next). Reproducer: wcstol_survey.

## 2026-07-04 (BlackThrush) — WIN (SHIPPED): wcstoul/wcstoull single-pass parse (1.66x -> 1.07x) — wide-int-parse family COMPLETE
Completes the wcstol single-pass campaign. wcstoul was still two-pass (1.662x vs glibc). Added `parse_wcstoul_fast` — the unsigned analog of `parse_wcstol_fast`, byte-identical to `wcstoul_impl` for base 10/16 (whitespace/sign/0x-prefix, checked-arithmetic overflow → u64::MAX + ERANGE, glibc's `-`-negation via `acc.wrapping_neg()`); base 0/other fall back to the two-pass core. Wired into wcstoul (wcstoull delegates to it → auto-fixed). **Deployed re-measure (`wcstol_survey`): wcstoul 1234567890 1.662 -> 1.074 (22.28ns -> 14.51ns).** CONFORMANCE GREEN (byte-exact vs glibc): wcstol_family_differential_fuzz. The wide integer parse family is now COMPLETE: wcstol ~par (1.09-1.24x, hex 0.556x WIN), wcstoll ~1.24x, wcstoul ~1.07x, wcstoull (delegates) — all from the original 1.4-2.1x LOSS to parity/win via single-pass parsers mirroring the deployed narrow fast paths. Reproducer: wcstol_survey.

## 2026-07-04 (BlackThrush) — WIN (SHIPPED): strtof_impl skips the redundant f64 double-parse (wcstof 1.3-1.44x LOSS -> 0.74-0.90x WIN)
`wcstod_survey`: wcstof LOSES 1.30-1.44x to glibc on every decimal while wcstod (same wide wrapper, same input) WINS — the difference is the core `strtof_impl` vs `strtod_impl`. Root: `strtof_impl` called the FULL `strtod_impl` (correct-rounded f64 parse) FIRST, purely to get `consumed`, THEN re-parsed the decimal as f32 via `parse_decimal_f32_prefix` — a double parse. LEVER: bound the token with a cheap `max_float_token_len` (maximal `ws sign 0-9.eE+-` run) and parse the f32 natively, skipping the f64 parse; falls through to the unchanged full path on any miss. SAFE & byte-identical: `parse_decimal_f32_prefix` rejects hex/inf/nan and Rust's `parse::<f32>` rejects over-consumed junk (both → full path), and on success the run is exactly a valid float so its length == strtod's consumed. GOTCHA (caught by the signbit fuzz, fixed): a `0x`/`0X` hex-float prefix stops the scan at `x`, hiding the prefix from `parse_decimal_f32_prefix` (mis-parsed "0x…p…" as bare "0") — added an explicit hex-prefix bail at the token start so hex floats take the strtod_impl path. **Deployed re-measure (`wcstod_survey`): wcstof 3.14159 1.299->0.774, 2.5 1.401->0.743, 123456.789 1.436->0.895 — all LOSS -> WIN.** Also speeds narrow strtof (shared core). CONFORMANCE GREEN (byte-exact vs glibc): strtod_strtof_signbit_differential_fuzz (200k, incl hex) + strtod_strtof_live_differential_probe + conformance_diff_strtod_edges + wcstod_wide_differential_fuzz + conformance_diff_wcstod + conformance_diff_stdlib_numeric (31). NOTE: wcstod "0" still 2.43x (the wide_parse_float scan+project wrapper overhead for trivial tokens — a separate wide-fast-path lever, not strtof_impl). Reproducer: wcstod_survey.

## 2026-07-04 (BlackThrush) — WIN (SHIPPED): wcstod plain-integer fast path (26.3ns -> 3.5ns, 2.4x LOSS -> 0.32x WIN)
`wcstod_survey`: wcstod WINS most decimals but LOSES 2.4x on "0" (26.3ns vs glibc 11ns) — fl has a ~constant ~26ns floor (scan_w_float_window + ASCII projection + strtod_impl) while glibc fast-paths trivial tokens. (First tried killing the `[0u8;512]` ascii_stack zero-init via MaybeUninit — ~0-gain, LLVM already elides it; DISCARDED.) LEVER: `parse_wcstod_integer_fast` — a single-pass path for a PLAIN base-10 integer exactly representable in f64 (`[ws][sign][digits]`, |v| ≤ 2^53, stop char not continuing the number via `.eExXpP`), returning the exact `acc as f64` (signed, `-0.0` preserved for "-0"); `None` for floats/hex/inf-nan/>2^53 → the unchanged full path. Exact integers never set ERANGE, so wcstod just writes endptr and returns. **Deployed re-measure (`wcstod_survey`): wcstod "0" 2.400 -> 0.319 (26.29ns -> 3.51ns, 3x faster than glibc); decimals unchanged (3.14159 0.98x, 2.5 0.95x, 1e-300 0.34x).** CONFORMANCE GREEN (byte-exact vs glibc incl. -0 signbit): strtod_strtof_signbit_differential_fuzz (200k) + wcstod_wide_differential_fuzz + conformance_diff_wcstod + conformance_diff_stdlib_numeric (31). FOLLOW-UP: "-1.5e10" still 1.71x (exact scientific value — needs an exact-mantissa Clinger/Eisel-Lemire fast path in core strtod_impl, deeper/riskier). Reproducer: wcstod_survey.

## 2026-07-04 (BlackThrush) — WIN (SHIPPED): atof delegates to strtod (2.75x LOSS -> 0.73x WIN)
`strtod_survey`: narrow strtod WINS every case (0.33-0.71x — it has the single-pass decimal/integer fast paths, incl. exact scientific "-1.5e10" 0.446x), but `atof` LOSES 2.749x on "0" (24.21ns vs glibc 8.8ns) and is ~par on "42". Root: fl's atof did `scan_terminated_numeric_string` + `core::atof` (= `strtod_impl(s).0`) — the SLOW general path — instead of strtod's fast paths. FIX: `atof(s) == strtod(s, NULL)` (the C definition, and exactly how glibc implements it), inheriting strtod's fast paths AND its glibc-matching ERANGE errno (glibc atof is strtod internally, so it DOES set errno — the old fl atof set none, a latent divergence this also closes). Byte-identical value (strtod's fast paths == strtod_impl == core::atof). **Deployed re-measure (`strtod_survey`): atof "0" 2.749 -> 0.727 (24.21ns -> 5.20ns), "42" 1.192 -> 0.336, "3.14159" 0.763 -> 0.473 — all WIN.** CONFORMANCE GREEN (byte-exact vs glibc): conformance_diff_stdlib_numeric (31) + stdlib_abi_test + conformance_diff_strtoimax + conformance_diff_strtod_edges. No test asserted atof leaves errno untouched (the C "need not affect errno" clause), so matching glibc's errno is safe. Reproducer: strtod_survey.

## 2026-07-04 (BlackThrush) — WIN (SHIPPED): wcstod routes projected ASCII through strtod's short-decimal fast path (-1.5e10 1.71x LOSS -> 1.14x)
Completing the wcstod story (integer fast path shipped 2 turns ago; narrow strtod already wins all via `parse_strtod_short_decimal_c_string_fast`). Remaining wide gap: exact scientific/short decimals ("-1.5e10" 1.714x, since wcstod called the SLOW `strtod_impl` on the projected ASCII, bypassing the narrow fast path). LEVER: the wide→ASCII projection is already NUL-terminated, so wcstod's `wide_parse_float` parse-closure now tries `parse_strtod_short_decimal_c_string_fast` on it FIRST (made pub(crate)), falling to `strtod_impl` on a miss. It only returns for exactly-representable values (mantissa ≤ 2^53, |exp| ≤ 400 — NEVER ERANGE, so erange=false); it is the SAME fast path narrow strtod uses over the SAME bytes → byte-identical. **Deployed re-measure (`wcstod_survey`): wcstod -1.5e10 1.714 -> 1.139 (44.6ns -> 29.9ns), 2.5 0.884 -> 0.814, 3.14159 0.977 -> 0.879, 123456.789 1.073 -> 1.022; 0 stays 0.322 WIN, 1e-300 0.383 WIN; wcstof unchanged (no regression).** wcstod now wins/pars EVERY surveyed case (only "inf" 1.22x — the special-value floor). CONFORMANCE GREEN (byte-exact vs glibc): strtod_strtof_signbit_differential_fuzz (200k) + wcstod_wide_differential_fuzz + conformance_diff_wcstod + conformance_diff_stdlib_numeric (31). The number-parse family (narrow+wide, int+float, atof) is now comprehensively at parity/win vs glibc. Reproducer: wcstod_survey.

## 2026-07-04 (BlackThrush) — WIN (SHIPPED): wcsftime stack buffers + ASCII bulk transcode (%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S 4.73x LOSS -> parity)
`wcsftime_survey`: wcsftime LOSES 4.7-31.8x to glibc on every format (all byte-exact). Root: it (1) heap-allocated `fmt_mb` Vec + `vec![0u8; maxsize*6]` and (2) transcoded the format wide→multibyte and the output multibyte→wide with a per-char `wctomb`/`mbtowc` codec dispatch. FIXES: (a) stack buffers for both (fmt 512B, out 1024B) with heap fallback only for large format/output (avoids the malloc) — ~20% alone; (b) ASCII fast paths in BOTH transcode loops: an ASCII wchar narrows 1:1 to its byte and an ASCII output byte widens 1:1 to its codepoint, skipping the codec dispatch (format + date output are almost always pure ASCII). **Deployed re-measure (`wcsftime_survey`): "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S" 4.731 -> 0.991 (271ns -> 56ns, PARITY — hits strftime's numeric_19 fast path), "%H:%M" 17.8 -> 12.0, "%A" 31.8 -> 20.4, "just text" 31.0 -> 14.6 (534ns -> 251ns).** CONFORMANCE GREEN (byte-exact vs glibc): conformance_diff_wcsftime_l. RESIDUAL: the still-losing formats ("%H:%M"/"%A"/"just text" 12-20x) are bottlenecked by the underlying strftime GENERAL LOOP (~40ns/directive, documented hard codegen problem) — they miss strftime's exact-string fast paths; wcsftime's own overhead is now gone (the fast-path format is parity). Reproducer: wcsftime_survey.

## 2026-07-04 (BlackThrush) — LOSS/DROP + BLOCKER: strftime general-loop cost is a ~200ns FIXED per-call overhead, NOT the literal push! loop
Chasing the strftime general-loop gap (last turn's flag: non-fast-path formats hit a flat ~190ns floor; it bottlenecks BOTH narrow strftime and wcsftime). HYPOTHESIS: the char-by-char `push!` (bounds-check + return-0 per byte) for literal runs was the cost → replace with a bulk `copy_from_slice` of each literal run. DISPROVEN (clean deployed A/B, `strftime_litrun_ab`, byte-exact via strftime_specifier + wide-year fuzz): narrow strftime "just text no directives" (24 chars) = 205ns, a 69-char literal = 220ns — only **~0.3ns/char**, so the per-char push! was NEVER the cost; the ~200ns is a FIXED per-call overhead paid by ANY format that misses the exact-string fast paths (hms/numeric_19/ymd). "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S" (fast path) = 20ns; "%H:%M"/"%A"/bare-text all ~200-220ns regardless of content/length. So the literal-run copy is ~0-gain → DROPPED (and was clobbered by shared-tree churn before commit anyway). The ~200ns is NOT: an allocation in format_strftime (all Vec/scratch are in conditional case/composite paths), read_tm/read_tm_zone (paid by the 20ns fast-path format too), or the loop body (~0.3ns/char). MOST LIKELY: codegen of the giant `format_strftime` (50-arm match + macro expansions) — non-inlined call + frame/register pressure on the general-loop path, which the fast-path formats escape via inlined small fns. Closing it needs splitting the general loop into its own function or a structural rewrite — a big swing, deferred. BLOCKER: strftime is currently PEER-CONTESTED (an active agent is A/B-ing `strftime_core_literal`/`strftime_abi_literal` in the shared `strftime_survey.rs`; my uncommitted time/mod.rs edit was reverted by their churn) — coordinate before touching format_strftime. Reproducer: strftime_litrun_ab.

## 2026-07-04 (BlackThrush) — WIN (SHIPPED): __wcsftime_l delegates to optimized wcsftime (was the pre-fix ~5-32x-glibc structure) + multibyte correctness fix
While wcsftime_l already delegated to the optimized `wcsftime` (stack buffers + ASCII bulk transcode, shipped last turn), the internal `__wcsftime_l` symbol had its OWN separate, UNOPTIMIZED body: it heap-allocated `vec![0u8; max*4]` per call + `wide_to_narrow` Vec, and — worse — widened the multibyte strftime output byte-by-byte (`b as wchar_t`), which is WRONG for any non-ASCII output (wcsftime decodes it with mbtowc). FIX: `__wcsftime_l` now delegates to `wcsftime` exactly like `wcsftime_l`, inheriting all its optimizations (and the peer's newer wcsftime commits). Since __wcsftime_l == wcsftime in the C locale, its perf is now wcsftime's: last turn measured "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S" 4.7x->0.99x (271ns->56ns, parity); the old __wcsftime_l had the SAME pre-fix heap-alloc+per-char-transcode structure (~5-32x glibc). This is BOTH a large speedup AND a correctness fix (non-ASCII output). CONFORMANCE GREEN (byte-exact vs glibc): conformance_diff_wcsftime_l. NOTE: this area (wcsftime/strftime/time_abi) is actively peer-contested — the shared worktree repeatedly reverted my uncommitted edits and deleted untracked bench files this session, so committed the code change immediately. Reproducer basis: wcsftime perf from the prior wcsftime commit (strftime_survey / wcsftime_survey).

## 2026-07-04 (BlackThrush) — BLOCKER (measured): swprintf loses 4.9x (%d) / 15.8x (%ls) to glibc — narrow-render round-trip is architectural
Surveyed deployed fl swprintf vs glibc (dlmopen LM_ID_NEWLM) with fixed-arg signatures, byte-identity confirmed both cases (match=true). `L"%d"` with 12345: fl=103.04ns glibc=20.88ns = **4.936x LOSS**. `L"%ls"` with L"hello world": fl=268.56ns glibc=16.99ns = **15.807x LOSS**. ROOT CAUSE (architectural, not a micro-lever): fl renders the WIDE printf family through the NARROW render engine — swprintf does `wide_to_narrow_pooled(format)` → `parse_format_string` → `render_wprintf`/`render_segments` (produces UTF-8 bytes) → `narrow_to_wide_buf` (decodes UTF-8 back to wchar_t). For `%ls` this is triple work on the argument: `wide_cstr_to_utf8(ptr,...)` (stdio_abi.rs:4616) ALLOCATES a fresh Vec<u8> and transcodes the wchar_t* arg wide→UTF-8, `format_str` copies it into the render buf, then `narrow_to_wide_buf` decodes UTF-8→wide AGAIN — where glibc's native `vfwprintf` copies the wide string directly with no transcode. The `%d` 4.9x is the same round-trip's fixed overhead (transcode "%d" wide→narrow, parse, positional-plan scan, render, decode "12345" narrow→wide) vs glibc's single-pass wide render. Output-buffer pooling was already landed by the printf campaign (99de4dee3), so the remaining gap is the transcode round-trip itself. Closing it requires a NATIVE wide render path (render directly into wchar_t, no narrow intermediary) — a large change to the printf-campaign's shared render_segments core, which is PEER-CONTESTED (active printf/scanf perf campaign owns stdio_abi render machinery). Deferred/coordinate before touching. Reproducer: `crates/frankenlibc-bench/examples/swprintf_survey.rs` (committed).

## 2026-07-10 (cod_fl) - REJECT: bd-dcrhgl existing-table guarded inline header is safe but slower (17.57ns -> 18.29ns)

- **NEGATIVE-EVIDENCE FIRST:** this is a follow-up to the 2026-07-04 bd-dcrhgl inline-header rejection above. The unsafe simulated header path remains attractive, but the previous rejection still holds: `known_remaining(addr)` accepts arbitrary ABI pointers, so production may not read `ptr[-16]` after only a coarse min/max range guard. The retry predicate required a no-fault exact-membership proof before any header read.
- **PROFILED BEFORE LEVER:** deployed ABI probe, local release-perf binary from `malloc_st_probe`, `perf stat -r 3`: `MALLOC_FREE` rows stayed at fl **38.30-40.43 ns** vs host glibc **4.57-4.82 ns** (`fl/glibc=8.304-8.484`), elapsed **1.4605s +/-0.92%**, cycles **5.851782046e9 +/-0.55%**, instructions **2.2795456258e10 +/-0.03%**. Flamegraph (`/tmp/frankenlibc-bd-dcrhgl-malloc-st.svg`) ranked `free` **13.20%**, `enter_allocator_reentry_guard` **11.25%**, plus visible `fallback_insert_sized_index`, `fallback_remove_sized_for_slot`, `record_alloc_stats`, and `record_free_stats`; kernel symbols were restricted, userspace stacks were usable.
- **MEASURED (`rch exec`, worker `ovh-a`, command: `cargo run --profile release-perf -p frankenlibc-bench --example malloc_sizetrack_ab --features abi-bench`):** extended the existing de-risking bench with `SIZETRACK_GUARDED_HEADER_AB`, which reads simulated header metadata only after `fallback_size_for_bench(p).is_some()` proves exact-start membership without dereferencing `p`. Same run: `GUARD_AB reentry_enter+exit=7.45ns/call`, `SIZETRACK_AB table=17.57ns header=0.93ns header/table=0.053 table_saves=16.65ns/op`, `SIZETRACK_GUARDED_HEADER_AB table=17.57ns guarded_header=18.29ns guarded/table=1.041 guarded_tax=0.71ns/op`, `STATS_AB record_alloc+free=16.53ns/alloc+free-pair`.
- **VERDICT:** reject "reuse the current fallback table as the no-fault proof, then read the inline header" as a production speed lever. It is safe, but it is **4.1% slower** than the current table lifecycle because the table proof is still paid before the header read. No allocator behavior changed; the landed increment is a proof/measurement harness row only.
- **RETRY CONDITION:** do not retry existing-table guarded headers.
